channel.title: Comments for page "Allison Eckhart"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Allison Eckhart"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Global Allison Eckhart Commission

LOL

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, you smurfed my smurf in your smurf-cp!

…wait, that smurfed out wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great, and I'm surprised that's it's not more highly rated considering how often I see it referred to in other places.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally, an article where 'just incinerate the anomalous byproducts' goes wrong! All the ash and gasses were Allison Eckhart, so when they got into the water cycle, all the water they touched became Allison Eckhart, creating a cascade effect. Now the beaches are covered in Allison Eckhart and Allison Eckhart falls from the skies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just imagine being this lady. Everything she touches becomes her. I’m thinking, if she has a spouse, they could be in love with the kitchen sink or something, thinking it’s her. Damn, no one even knows where she is anymore.
Oh, my trash semantic daughter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this might be something of a necropost, but I fully agree with everything you just said. However, the lack of me understanding what the hell was going on was what sealed my downvote. In order for me to understand the article in the first place, I had to go down into the discussion and read the version left by the author. Sorry, but if I have to do that in order to understand the article, you should probably change something about that.

Honestly, while the idea itself is at least interesting, the amounts of repitition in this article just make me lose any and all interest that I could have had in the article.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now imagine if a woman named Allison Eckhart was to google her name and found this.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: TriStar Pictures would be this too except the author didn't change the page's title (don't know why).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps they just went insane since they perceived the water as being Allison Eckhart, the horror of entire oceans simultaneously being a single human caused them to go insane or go into a coma or whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since there's a big risk of the entire Earth becoming Allison Eckhart, it is a VERY GOOD IDEA to stop it, however the article says there's a risk of everything infecting becoming conceptually null instead of returning to normal. Even with that, it is worth a try, since if they evacuate Earth with the population which is left, the Earth would eventually be infected anyways, and what if it propagates to the moon and maybe even other planets? The SCP Foundation should try to destroy it as soon as possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think it's a problem. There are some format-screw articles on the side but they're hard to write so there will not be too many of these. If there were too many people would start downvoting them thus making creation of more unwanted, so there's no risk of the site being overrun. However I think it should be edited so that it's more clear what's going on (without reading the unaltered version in the comments), and maybe make it Neutralized, there was a neutralization idea in the article, maybe make it successful would be a good idea. Even without that, I still like this one very much.

item.title: Re: Allison Eckhart
item.content:encoded: I concur. At base it was clear that AE-ness was communicable to other things and concepts, but the fact the vector was her anatomy and byproducts wasn't even vaguely evident.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I freakin love conceptual/semantic SCPs, simply because of the association of logic/semantics' underlying structure being somehow a tangible thing that can be affected by earthly phenomena.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Allison Eckhart is SCP-25645, ergo reference to 2565 becomes Allison Eckhart. However, she is merely a part of the human race. A part of the whole becomes the whole, but the human race is not a part of Allison Eckhart, but vice versa, ergo it does not become her.

item.title: Re: Allison Eckhart
item.content:encoded:
I think that you should edit it so that the waste products part is more obvious and we don't have to use this to understand it. For example

Because of this, Allison Eckhart's internal Allison Eckharts and Allison Eckharts excreted from Allison Eckhart also possess the property of "Allison Eckhart" and are thus perceived as Allison Eckhart.

Maybe keep the adjectives and only change the nouns to Allison Eckhart so that it'll be easier to guess while keeping the anomalous effect. I up-voted nonetheless, but it might get you more votes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, they died because they drank Allison Eckhart. Don't drink human beings.

I don't want to clog up the discussion page too much, but this is what I don't understand about the logic of the skip. Yes, from the frame of reference of these people, they drank Allison Eckhart. But that is a semantic/semiotic confusion on their part. From our frame of reference, what they drank was water - they just perceived it as Allison Eckhart. So why did they die?

If you had stated that people had died of dehydration, rather than commit cannibalism by drinking Allison Eckhart, then I would be closer to understanding. But in the same way that I would not die (or be a cannibal) if I ate SCP-1539-03, despite the fact that Agent Jim Thayer has been dead for some time, I don't understand how people here are dying from drinking Allison Eckhart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So are these people dying because of the chaos caused by this skip?

No, they died because they drank Allison Eckhart. Don't drink human beings. This has been a PSA.

Not sure what this really means. Lack of any real identity towards anything?

Well, if her blood is Allison Eckhart, and her dead skin flakes are Allison Eckhart, then her breath would also be Allison Eckhart. Hence Breathability scenario, because she could literally be clouding the world up with Allison Eckhart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few things but I may be a bit dense about this.

Following continuing civilian casualties due to ingestion of SCP-2565-contaminated water

So are these people dying because of the chaos caused by this skip?

total AEK-Class Allison Eckhart End-of-the-World Breathability Scenario

Not sure what this really means. Lack of any real identity towards anything?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding kaktus here. Judge a mainlister as a mainlister, not as a -J. Also, not everything that has a little bit of humor in it necessarily has to be a -J (and of course, not everyone finds every -J humorous). If we only limited our articles to being grimdark and spooky, we'd miss out on some good stories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wholeheartedly disagree. Just because something is funny doesn't mean it's a joke, and this wouldn't pass as a -J article at all. What we should judge it as is what the author intended it to be, not what we think it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Oh. Switching from neutral- to upvote now that I know what the hell is going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I can see another reason I dislike the crosslinking thing: You have to go back and read entire fucking articles to understand the one you're currently reading. I didn't have that problem here — I remembered both of those easily enough once I saw the first lines of the descriptions — but that could get really annoying in the future. Not downvote-worthy, though.

Still, no vote on the grounds that A) this is nearly impossible to follow, B) the escalation revealed by the author's note is frankly ludicrous, C) it's like Inside crossed with… Well, any of a couple of humanoid skips I can think of. And is overall too much like Inside and Di Molte Volci for my tastes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure how the anomaly knows when to stop, so to speak. If the SCP designation itself is now perceived as Allison Eckhart because she is SCP-2565, then wouldn't humanity, thus genus Homo, family Hominidae, etc up to Animalia also be perceived as Allison Eckhart? She is part of humanity, and even the smallest part is perceived as the whole. Even regret expressed from someone perceived as Allison Eckhart is now perceived as Allison Eckhart. It can be taken further such as she's an earthling and Earth is part of the Solar System which is part of the Milky Way that is part of the Universe..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the GAEC to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And of course the GOC wants to destroy it.

That tipped me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. I'd say that's a reasonable enough reaction.

One way in which they differ is the direction in which I take it - while the SCP, which used to be a library, is hiding something within it (or at least that was what I got out of it), this Allison Eckhart is unintentionally trying to take over the world by still existing.

Allison Eckhart is also pretty heavily focussed around the semantic fuckery of being the water you drink, a cloud over the pacific ocean, a couple million people, and all of those people's skin flakes, hair, bones and assorted organs, so in that sense it's more like the Cone of Humanity.

The SCP that used to be a library, Di Molte Volci and Allison Eckhart all mess with the title of their SCP by virtue of their anomalous properties - in the case of Allison Eckhart, it's because the SCP designation is part of Allison Eckhart, and hence affected by Allison Eckhart's anomaly (i.e. becomes Allison Eckhart).

but that's what I plan to do, and what I imagine this author will do

Yup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Allison Eckhart would probably win if only in numbers, although with regards to interaction there'd be one hell of a lot of cognitive dissonance. Think you're Allison Eckhart one moment, Sarah Palmer the next…

Go write a tale about it before someone removes that allison-eckhart tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The problem here is that every part of Allison Eckhart has become a threat to making sure you stay conceptually sane and healthy. The whole concept of auto-synecdoche means that any smaller part of Allison Eckhart becomes the whole. Hence why she's in a biohazard cell, because if so much as a skin flake of her gets out, that immediately becomes Allison Eckhart. If she accidentally coughs on someone, they'd become Allison Eckhart (hence "contaminated with Allison Eckhart".)

Imagine how you'd react if you suddenly lost all self-identity and could only think of yourself in terms of Allison Eckhart. Now extend that feeling to everyone who came into contact with a single skin flake, drop of saliva or nail clipping and you get the reason they're not letting her run about.

ETA: Sanitised version published in the Author Post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neither depart from standard titling nearly as much as these two.

If I was confident that there's more to my issues than just personal insecurity, I would have downvoted… I'll come back in a month or so and give this another read, to see if my reaction has passed. I'll vote then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I won't necessarily fault you for the lapses in internal logic that this piece has, which have been largely handled by psul already. It's certainly more grounded in something resembling meaning than the "Inside" article that this references. But the main event, saying "Allison Eckhart" a bunch of times in nonsensical contexts, isn't really doing much for me, so then I find myself going back to those lapses as I read it.

Then again, I'm not sure I'd like this much more if it did obey a more rigid sense of logical consistency.

It's not actively obnoxious (which is kind of strange, given how many times "Allison Eckhart" appears in this article), so I'm not necessarily of the "nuke it from orbit" opinion on this one, but it's leaving me pretty cold, which ultimately guides my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be fair, non-standard mainlist titles aren't new. To a more subtle extent we've had two (and maybe more I don't recall immediately) for a couple years now. http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-2557 and http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-2930 . 2557 is arguable. SCCP-2930 isn't. Off the top of my head SCP-2521 *should* be nonstandardly named but wasn't.

Even if this isn't as good as yours (which I can agree with, though I've upvoted both), maybe the next one will be better than both. I'll take that even if it means we get a bunch of mediocre (which, in my opinion, this is not), pieces in the mean time. The best versions of something are very rarely the first ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a form of convergent memetic evolution?

This would be my guess, though I think it's less being jaded to the format and more seeking novelty.

I also think the overlap is at least partly the logical result of following two meta-anomalies to their logical conclusions: Yours, from what I understand, replaces consensus definitions/descriptions with the discussion used to arrive at consensus in any case where there is any dissenting discussion. This one spreads its semantic identity and name to anything associated with it.

Different anomalies, same end result: neither can be properly numbered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think 'jaded' might be the wrong term — if that were the case, you wouldn't expect people to go back to writing more traditional SCPs afterwards, but that's what I plan to do, and what I imagine this author will do. It's more like experimentation, and while it has its downsides, I normally won't complain about it until the gimmick in question gets annoying.

My problem here is that it comes so close to my own work (it's like my own SCP-2602, which used to be a library, with one of the big stylistic flourishes of Di Molte Voci) and so soon after my own work. It's more personal, and it leaves a bad taste in my mouth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It definitely felt like the building that used to be a library with less effort and more repetition, but it was still a fun read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps the issue is that too many authors are getting jaded of the standard format, and thus feel compelled to break it in as many ways as possible, causing a form of convergent memetic evolution?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even after reading the comments, there is a lot here that I don't understand. And to second psul:

As far as semantic satiation goes, this is more of a Buffalo buffalo than a former library or a body in the water.

There's that, and there's also the fact that I'm not really a fan of Foundation employees becoming skips. E-class is a thing, even though it's a thing that gets forgotten a lot; that said, maybe she actually is anomalous enough on her own and not just anomalously affected, and thus does deserve SCP classification, but I'm not seeing it through the aforementioned word confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't a fan — not interested, didn't feel terribly intriguing, basically agreed with psul. I'm not going to downvote though — this comes hot on the heels of my own infohazard that, in addition to being meta, was unique for how it doesn't have a standard mainlist title, for all of a few days.

This feels a lot like a copycat — I've commented before on how cool and original things get diluted by a lot of mediocre SCPs until it's just another formatting trick that you get tired of seeing, and I had a negative gut reaction when I saw this article do it to my own. And that's the problem — I'm worried that because it's my SCP's uniqueness that's at stake, I'm not being fair… but I can't not comment on it y'know? So just, keep this in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why, maybe I'm just a sucker for format screws, but I liked this for some reason. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With articles based around a joke, the question I ask is: Would they work as well or better as -Js? If they could, why are they on the mainlist?

The Ramjet Cows (SCP-2008) are an extended deadpan joke. So is the toaster (SCP-426). But they wouldn't work as actual Joke articles. This one, I think, would. So that's what it should be, and what we should judge it as.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it. As I was reading, I was actually thinking that if this started spreading (however that actually happens), it would spiral out of control almost immediately. So imagine my delight when that's exactly what happens. I honestly upvoted quite early at the phrase "autosynecdochic semantic pointer, believed to be the result of an artificially induced conceptual fractal" and dared the entry to reverse my vote as I kept reading. Didn't happen!

This entry did remind me quite a bit of SCP-2310, and now I wonder if there was any way for those two situations to come head-to-head, who would overcome the other?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If the suits and the cells are perceived to be women, then the idea of wearing them or containing people inside them would be abhorrent, not form part of the containment procedures.

*looks at my Halloween avatar*
*smirks*

The odd thing is, I could swear that I've seen Allison Eckhart elsewhere before…

(Memo to self: Googling Allison Eckhart after reading this article is rather disconcerting.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an absolute slog to get through with the nigh-constant repetition, though I have no clue how you could rework it given the affect it has. Skits by with a no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My logicking, may be bullshit:

If the suits and the cells are perceived to be women, then the idea of wearing them or containing people inside them would be abhorrent, not form part of the containment procedures.

Allison Eckhart, as in SCP-2565 herself, already contains herself, because she contains the organs, skin cells, blood and brains of Allison Eckhart: all of which reduce themselves to Allison Eckhart via the logic of the skip.

Since the Site Director is also an Allison Eckhart themselves, and the staff using the HAZMAT suits are Allison Eckharts, there would be nothing unusual to either Allison Eckhart, because Allison Eckhart has been containing Allison Eckharts ever since the initial anomaly manifested.

deepest Allison Eckharts

In this case, we replace "Allison Eckhart" with "regret". Allison Eckhart can embody "regret", or "fear" or "joy" much like a political figure can embody "hope" or "grief".

100 liters of Allison Eckhart

Liters are admittedly more frequently used to describe liquids as opposed to people, but what would this imply about the state of Allison Eckhart?

Would you want to drink a human being pulped into 100 liters of juice like so much CapriSun?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy marklar!

I don't think this is "bad". I mean, the anomaly takes the role of the standard expungement to create mistery, but then I agree with Jack Ike that it gets annoying at some point. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quickly, go write four tales with another author and we can get a legit character tag going!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As far as semantic satiation goes, this is more of a Buffalo buffalo than a former library or a body in the water. I appreciate the idea of cross-linking, but those two very strong articles merely show up the weakness of this one.

I'm not convinced by the logic of the anomaly. If every part of this woman, and everything connected with her, is perceived to be her, then it makes no sense to refer to "Allison Eckhart Containment Cells" or "Level Allison ECKHART suits". If the suits and the cells are perceived to be women, then the idea of wearing them or containing people inside them would be abhorrent, not form part of the containment procedures.

Similarly, later references to "deepest Allison Eckharts" and "100 liters of Allison Eckhart" don't make a huge amount of sense to me - those aren't phrases that one would use to refer to a person, so I don't think the semiotics works.

So unless I'm missing something in the logic, this just becomes another exercise in "skip that affects its own documentation", of which it is merely a fair example of something already done very well. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I appreciate the joke, "allison-eckhart" isn't a real tag, and since this article looks like it'll do well, that tag should probably go.

item.title: Allison Eckhart
item.content:encoded:
Allison Eckhart Allison Eckhart Allison Eckhart - Allison Eckhart Allison Eckhart 'Allison Eckhart' Allison Eckhart, Allison Eckhart Allison Eckhart.

-Allison Eckhart

(I get what the anomaly is, but it obfuscates the story to an irritating degree.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do like the use of the Semantic Dissociation Field article here; and the effects are pretty much clear enough without being overly cloaked in technical language. One gripe is that the escalation feels a bit rushed: I'm having to consider the end of the world, a Broken Allison Eckhart Scenario, and a Foundation with the capacity for massive offworld relocation within the span of a few hundred words. It might be just me, but certain revelations necessitate certain styles of delivery in my book. Djoric's DEER nails it; this one doesn't really hit the mark.

that said, this was a fun read. Have a ++.

item.title: Allison Eckhart
item.content:encoded:
synecdoche, n.

a figure of speech in which a part is made to represent the whole or vice versa, as in England lost by six wickets (meaning ‘ the English cricket team’).

The title is intentional.

Let's see how many hours this lasts on the mainsite.

+ Allison Eckhart Minus Allison Eckhart
– hide block

Item #: SCP-2565

Object Class: Keter

Special Containment Procedures: Until such a time in which Experimental Cross-Containment Proposal 2565/2719 or Neutralisation Proposal SCP-2565 can be enacted, SCP-2565 and SCP-2565 instances are currently contained in seperate Biohazardous Humanoid Containment Cells at Bio Site-18. As per standard biosecurity procedures, personnel entering the containment cells of SCP-2565 are to wear Level A HAZMAT suits in order to avoid becoming contaminated with SCP-2565.

Currently, SCP-2565's waste products are to be stored in sealed steel containers and buried underground at Secure Disposal Area 18 to avoid contamination or containment breach of SCP-2565.

Description: SCP-2565 is Agent Allison Eckhart, a 31-year old woman who was formerly a field operative for the Department of Analytics. Aside from a minor excess of sodium concentration in its blood, SCP-2565 does not appear to possess any physical anomalies. Prior to containment, Allison Eckhart had worked for 5 years as a Level 2 Anomalous Object Recovery agent. SCP-2565 did not possess anomalous properties during this time period.

SCP-2565 is an autosynecdochic semantic pointer, believed to be the result of an artificially induced conceptual fractal. Because of this, SCP-2565's internal organs and waste products also possess the property of "Allison Eckhart" and are thus perceived as Allison Eckhart. As an example, simply attempting to take blood samples from SCP-2565 has led to over three hundred complaints to the Ethics Committee from researchers assigned to SCP-2565.

Addendum Allison Eckhart-1: Members of GoI-139 ("[NAME HERE]") carried out an attack on Secure Disposal Area 18, presumably for the purpose of obtaining SCP-2565's waste products. Due to the small size of the group, it is believed that all members were contaminated in the process. During the attack, 36 security guards and 13 processing personnel were also contaminated. Their remains have been incinerated to avoid further spread of SCP-2565.

Site Director [NAME HERE] has expressed deepest regrets over the casualties involved in the incident. The families of the casualties involved have been given appropriate reimbursement for SCP-2565 conversion.

Addendum Allison Eckhart-2: Embedded Foundation assets in meteorology offices report multiple SCP-2565-contaminated cloud formations over the Pacific Ocean, suggesting the successful atmospheric dispersal of SCP-2565's waste products. As a result, over 100 liters of SCP-2565-contaminated water has been dispersed over the coastline of Australia.

Following continuing civilian casualties due to ingestion of SCP-2565-contaminated water, a Broken Masquerade scenario has been established due to the high-publicity nature of the casualties. It is estimated that at SCP-2565's current rate of waste product emission, a total AEK-Class Allison Eckhart End-of-the-World Breathability Scenario will occur within 40 years.

Plans for off-Earth evacuation of the remaining non-SCP-2565 population have already been set up, although the Global Occult Coalition has also proposed a plan for destruction of SCP-2565. It is believed that this proposal would carry a non-zero risk of an SK-Class Total Semantic Failure Scenario along with the neutralisation of SCP-2565.
